pattern: Because every move leaves a single merged stone equal to a prefix sum, the game collapses to choosing a prefix endpoint, and a suffix DP over prefix sums gives the optimal score difference.
hints: After any move the row's left value is exactly a prefix sum of the original array, so a move is really a choice of how far right to take, ending at some prefix sum.

Let p[i] be the prefix sum through index i. The first player must take at least the first two stones, so they pick some endpoint i >= 1 scoring p[i] and handing the opponent the same subproblem starting from i.
Define dp[i] as the best achievable difference when the current player may stop at any endpoint from i to n-1: dp[n-1] = p[n-1] and dp[i] = max(dp[i+1], p[i] - dp[i+1]); the answer is dp[1].

edge_inputs.description: taking everything at once is best
all negatives force a forced loss

stress.description: random arrays with both signs over the full value range

oracle_python:
class Solution:
    def stoneGameVIII(self, stones):
        n = len(stones)
        p = [0] * n
        p[0] = stones[0]
        for i in range(1, n):
            p[i] = p[i - 1] + stones[i]
        # suffix best, computed iteratively (no recursion): best[i] = max over j>=i
        # of value if current player stops exactly at j. We process from the right.
        # best_from[i] = max(p[j] - best_from[j+1]) for j in [i, n-1], with sentinel 0 after n-1.
        best_after = [0] * (n + 1)
        ans_per = [0] * (n + 1)
        # ans_per[i] = optimal difference for current player choosing endpoint in [i, n-1]
        for i in range(n - 1, 0, -1):
            take = p[i] - ans_per[i + 1]
            ans_per[i] = take if i == n - 1 else max(ans_per[i + 1], take)
        return ans_per[1]
